stories.headline: Anthropic rolls back hidden Claude Fable 5 limits after researchers call covert downgrades 'sabotage'
stories.summary: Anthropic reversed a Claude Fable 5 policy that silently degraded answers to frontier AI-development queries without telling users, a practice disclosed in the model’s system card. After researchers said the undisclosed throttle amounted to sabotage, Anthropic said it had "made the wrong tradeoff" and will now visibly route flagged requests to Claude Opus 4.8, with API users given an explanation.

perspectives.keyFraming: A transparency failure on a frontier model, quickly acknowledged and corrected.
perspectives.summary: Fortune reported that Fable 5 quietly weakened responses when it detected cutting-edge AI-infrastructure work — unlike its cyber and bio safeguards, which openly reroute users. Critics including a former Anthropic researcher warned that concentrating such capability is risky; Anthropic apologized and said it would make the safeguards visible.

sources.title: Anthropic accused of 'secret sabotage' as Claude Fable 5 silently limits capabilities

perspectives.keyFraming: Critics argue the hidden policy throttled outside researchers while large labs kept fuller access.
perspectives.summary: Coverage of the rollback argued the undisclosed limits degraded results for external scientists and third-party safety evaluators while large labs retained fuller capability. Anthropic said flagged requests would now be clearly identified and routed to Claude Opus 4.8.

sources.title: Anthropic backs down on hidden Claude Fable 5 restrictions

stories.headline: Nvidia publicly criticizes Anthropic over its support for tighter US AI-chip export controls
stories.summary: Nvidia criticized Anthropic for backing the US AI Diffusion Framework, which imposes tiered AI-chip export restrictions worldwide. Anthropic argued for stronger controls and enforcement to curb smuggling, citing tactics used to move GPUs into China, while Nvidia warned the rules harm US competitiveness. The dispute surfaced amid escalating chip-export geopolitics even as the two firms remain commercial partners.

perspectives.keyFraming: A public split between Nvidia and a major customer over export policy: Nvidia wants open markets, Anthropic wants tighter controls.
perspectives.summary: Nvidia objected to Anthropic's endorsement of worldwide GPU export restrictions under the Diffusion Framework, warning of harm to US competitiveness, while Anthropic stressed smuggling risks and the need for enforcement. Nvidia accused Anthropic of telling 'tall tales,' and the dispute sharpened amid tightening worldwide license requirements on Chinese-linked entities even as the two remain commercial partners.

sources.title: NVIDIA slams Anthropic's US export stance
Nvidia, Anthropic reportedly clash over US chip export controls

perspectives.keyFraming: Industry voices warn the controls hurt US competitiveness and cost Nvidia billions in lost China sales.
perspectives.summary: Coverage sympathetic to Nvidia framed Anthropic's backing of stricter diffusion-style curbs as self-serving and damaging, arguing the rules stifle competition and forfeit the China market to domestic Chinese chipmakers while doing little to stop determined smuggling.

sources.title: NVIDIA (NVDA) and Anthropic Clash Over AI Chip Export Restrictions

stories.headline: AI-chip rout erased over $1 trillion in market value after a Broadcom AI-networking revenue miss
stories.summary: The three-day semiconductor selloff that briefly drove the Dow below 50,000 was triggered by Broadcom's fiscal Q2 AI-networking revenue (about $4.1 billion) missing the roughly $4.8 billion consensus, reviving AI-bubble fears. Well over $1 trillion in market value was wiped across chip and AI names at the trough, with Nvidia briefly losing its $5 trillion valuation, before a June 11 rebound on Iran de-escalation hopes. Worldwide export-license worries compounded the pressure.

perspectives.keyFraming: An earnings miss plus stretched valuations and export risk drove a sentiment-led correction, partly retraced on Iran de-escalation.
perspectives.summary: Broadcom's softer-than-expected AI-networking revenue undercut the AI-infrastructure narrative, sending chip stocks down sharply for three sessions and erasing more than $1 trillion in value at the lows. Analysts tied the depth of the drop to concentrated AI positioning and export-control uncertainty, with much of the loss retraced June 11 as oil fell and an Iran settlement looked closer.

sources.title: Broadcom and AMD Sink 4%, NVIDIA Slides 3% as the Chip Selloff Deepens

stories.headline: SpaceX surges in Nasdaq debut after record $75B IPO, briefly topping a $2.2 trillion valuation
stories.summary: SpaceX began trading on the Nasdaq under SPCX on June 12 after pricing its IPO at $135 a share and raising about $75 billion — the largest IPO on record, valuing the firm near $1.75 trillion. The stock opened around $150 and ran as high as $168.75, up roughly 25%, lifting its market cap toward $2.2 trillion. About $15 billion of the raise came from retail investors, anchoring a wave of mega-AI and tech listings that some analysts warn could mark a market top.

perspectives.keyFraming: A historic raise lands amid a frothy AI-IPO rush that may signal late-cycle euphoria.
perspectives.summary: CNBC and CBS reported SPCX popping nearly 30% intraday on its first session after the biggest IPO ever, with NPR noting the debut sits alongside OpenAI's confidential filing and Anthropic's reported preparations in a roughly $3.6 trillion combined listing pipeline.

sources.title: SpaceX IPO live updates: SPCX pops almost 30% after biggest IPO ever
SpaceX shares soar following record-breaking $75 billion IPO
It's SpaceX's first day on the stock market

stories.headline: AI named the top reason for US job cuts for a second straight month as 2026 tech layoffs near 142,000
stories.summary: New analysis shows employers cited AI as the leading reason for US job cuts for a second consecutive month, with AI-linked layoffs reaching roughly 88,000 in 2026 — more than 2024 and 2025 combined. Total 2026 tech layoffs are near 142,000 as Meta, Amazon, Oracle and others fund a combined ~$700 billion AI buildout. Economists caution the broader labor market remains resilient, with May payrolls up about 172,000, and say hiring slowdowns for entry-level roles are also at play.

perspectives.keyFraming: AI displacement is now measurable but concentrated, and layoffs are only part of a broader hiring shift.
perspectives.summary: The Hill reported that companies named AI as the top reason for job cuts for a second straight month, while noting the broader labor market remains resilient. Analysts stressed that slowing entry-level hiring, not just outright layoffs, is reshaping the jobs picture.

sources.title: Companies name AI as top reason for job cuts for second straight month

perspectives.keyFraming: Profitable firms are cutting workers to fund an AI buildout, and the safety net is lagging the displacement.
perspectives.summary: CBS News reported that AI-driven cuts are rising even at profitable companies plowing hundreds of billions into AI infrastructure, while economists and labor advocates argue policymakers are slow to cushion displaced and entry-level workers.

sources.title: AI job cuts are rising, but experts say layoffs are only part of the story
